narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40—above the typical 3.21 average—and an LSAT score of 36, which is close to the dataset average of 36.70, you've put together a respectable academic profile for law school admission. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your probability of failure at 51.8%, tipping the scales just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This prediction draws from patterns in data on thousands of students, focusing on factors like early academic performance, family background, and standardized test results to forecast bar success. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, well below the average of 5.53, signaling to the model that your later undergraduate performance placed you in the lowest group compared to peers, a strong red flag for bar exam struggles. Counterbalancing this somewhat was your LSAT score, which had a notable positive influence by nudging the prediction toward passing, as it aligns closely with successful students. Similarly, your above-average undergraduate GPA provided another boost, helping pull the odds slightly in favor of success despite other concerns. Your lower-middle family income bracket and full-time student status, common among applicants, played lesser roles here. Overall, while your LSAT and GPA showed promise, the model weighed your low Year 3 decile most heavily, viewing it as the biggest hurdle compared to typical students who rank much higher in later years and pass the bar more reliably. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male applicant—many in similar demographics succeed with stronger late-stage grades—but a data-driven call highlighting where your profile diverged from bar passers. To move forward, consider retaking courses or pursuing a post-baccalaureate program to boost your decile rankings, as improving later academic standings has flipped predictions for others in your position. You might also seek targeted LSAT prep or bar prediction tools to build a stronger case for reapplication. With focused effort on those weaker areas, you can reshape your profile to align better with admitted students who pass the bar on their first try.
